next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The Astronomy Tower was the coldest place in the castle and Gavin hadn't brought a cloak.

He sat on the stone floor with his back against the parapet, his school robes pulled tight around his chest, and felt the wind come through the gaps between the crenellations in bursts that flattened his hair and then died and came again. He'd been up here for a while. An hour, maybe. Long enough that his hands had stopped just being cold and had moved into the numb stage where the fingers didn't bend right when he tried to make a fist.

March in Scotland. The thaw had started but hadn't committed — patches of snow still on the mountains, the grounds mostly mud with green pushing through in places, the lake half-frozen and half-not in a way that looked like a decision the water couldn't make. From up here the Forbidden Forest was a dark shape pressed against the hills, and below it the lawns sloped down to Hagrid's cabin, which was dark. Hagrid might be in the forest tonight. Gavin didn't know. He hadn't paid attention to Hagrid's schedule in months. He hadn't paid attention to most things in months.

He'd come up because the sub-collection was too small. The walls of those cut-stone rooms had started to press. Fourteen weeks of working there — the limestone smell in his clothes, the faint glow of stones that predated everything else in the castle — and the air had begun to feel used. After dinner he'd meant to go back down to the barrel entrance and the common room but he'd kept walking. Up the stairs past the library landing. Up more stairs. Past the Dark Arts classroo...
open_hooks_to_carry: Duncan's torture in full: the sound, the body's response, the horror of watching someone you know in pain in a room of people who cannot stop it. This is the novel's hardest scene and it must not flinch

Gavin's moral failure: the question of whether he could intervene via the First Art, his assessment that he probably cannot (but does not try), and his awareness that the uncertainty is not the real reason for his inaction
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ll against his chest and looked at the barrel ceiling and understood, clearly and without comfort, that he was alone with this. That the work ahead — the wards, the substrate, the whole impossible scope of what the Builders had left behind — was his because there was nobody else. And the loneliness of it was not going to resolve. Not through sharing. Not through proof. Not through Miren's kindness or Babbling's expertise or anyone else's belief or disbelief. The gap between what he could see and what he could demonstrate was not a problem to be solved. It was the shape of his situation.

He put the quill on the nightstand. He cleaned the handkerchief with a Scourgify that mostly worked — his wand still did ordinary spells, and he was still mediocre at them — and the worst of the blood vanished, leaving a faint pinkish ghost on the cotton. He turned on his side. Through the round window he could see the January dark, the grounds invisible except for the faint light from Hagrid's cabin far below and the deeper black of the Forest beyond it, its edge just visible against the sky.

He closed his eyes. The last thing he was aware of before sleep was the quill on the nightstand, less visible than everything else in the room, existing in a state that he had made and could not share and would not be able to explain to anyone — not tomorrow, not ever, unless he found a way to give someone else the eyes to see it. It sat there through the dark hours while the castle settled around it, patient and unwitnessed, holding its changed shape in a world that did not know it had been changed.
state_deltas_required: The Carrow regime at its most brutal — Duncan tortured in front of the class — colliding with Gavin's growing First Art ability and his failure to use it

Moral — Duncan is tortured with the Cruciatus Curse in class. Gavin watches. He might be able to disrupt the curse via substrate editing. He does not try. The gap between his abilities and his moral engagement widens into the novel's central indictment
